---
title: "Research"
page-layout: full
---

## Research Topics

LASER develops computational and experimental methods to enable **autonomous systems engineering** — reducing reliance on manual expert iteration across the full lifecycle of process system design, operation, and validation.

Three fundamental challenges structure our research program. First, engineering decisions must satisfy hard physical, safety, and economic constraints; embedding these rigorously within learned or data-driven frameworks remains an open problem. Second, real process systems operate under deep uncertainty — in model parameters, disturbances, and structural assumptions — so decision-making must be robust by design. Third, informative data is structurally scarce in process operations: normal-state records dominate while fault events, rare operating modes, and distribution shifts are underrepresented, demanding data-efficient learning strategies that remain reliable beyond the training regime.

---

## Autonomy Pillars

### Pillar 1 — Autonomous Learning & Experimentation

**Problem:** Characterizing a new process, material, or operating condition typically requires many manually designed and individually evaluated experiments. Reducing this expert burden while maximizing information yield per experiment is a core challenge in both computational and physical domains.

**Key methods:** Design of experiments (DOE), response surface methodology (RSM), Bayesian optimization (BO), informative design of experiments (iDoE), active learning, sequential and model-based experimental design

**Artifacts:** Benchmark experimental design protocols, reproducible sequential workflow code, experiment decision logs

**Representative applications:** Automated experiment sequencing for electrochemical CO₂ reduction catalyst screening; active learning for photobioreactor operating condition characterization

---

### Pillar 2 — Autonomous Modeling & Decision-Making

**Problem:** Process systems combine first-principles structure with uncertain or empirical components. Building surrogate models that respect physical constraints, propagate uncertainty, and support downstream optimization — without requiring a new modeling effort per application — is a persistent bottleneck for autonomous workflows.

**Key methods:** Hybrid digital twins, physics-informed surrogate models, uncertainty-aware regression, constrained optimization, stochastic and robust programming, multi-objective decision-making under uncertainty

**Artifacts:** Validated hybrid model libraries, uncertainty quantification benchmarks, reusable constrained optimization templates

**Representative applications:** Hybrid surrogate-based optimization of membrane CO₂ separation processes; uncertainty-aware design of hydrogen production systems

---

### Pillar 3 — Autonomous Operation & Validation

**Problem:** Deploying a model or decision policy into an operating system requires a continuous validation layer — detecting when the model is wrong, when operating conditions have shifted, and when a recommended action would violate admissibility requirements. This layer must be embedded in the workflow architecture, not appended after deployment.

**Key methods:** Process monitoring and anomaly detection, fault detection and diagnosis, advanced process control, model predictive control (MPC), real-time optimization

**Validation & Decision Metrics:**

- **Decision objectives and constraints:** Techno-economic analysis (TEA), life cycle assessment (LCA), and life cycle costing (LCC) serve as formal objective functions and constraint checkers — not post-hoc annotations
- **Validator role:** TEA/LCA screens candidate process configurations against economic and sustainability thresholds before any deployment decision is made
- **Uncertainty layer:** Price volatility, equipment lifetime, and efficiency uncertainty are propagated through TEA/LCA models for risk-aware configuration selection

**Artifacts:** Monitoring and anomaly detection benchmarks, reproducible TEA/LCA workflow templates, validation protocol documentation

**Representative applications:** Real-time monitoring and fault detection in microalgae bioprocess systems; TEA-guided screening of carbon capture and utilization process configurations

---

## Iterative Workflow

The three pillars connect through a single **iterative, validation-driven workflow**. Rather than treating learning, modeling, and operation as separate engineering phases, LASER designs them as a coupled end-to-end cycle:

> **Observe → Model → Decide → Validate → Deploy & Monitor → Update**

At the **Validate** step, formal constraints — physical feasibility, economic viability (TEA/LCA), and risk bounds — are checked before any decision is deployed. The cycle repeats until validation criteria are satisfied. Feedback control is one component within the **Deploy & Monitor** step; it is not the defining identity of the workflow.

---

## Application Areas

LASER applies its autonomous workflow methods across process and industrial domains. Carbon-related systems appear as illustrative cases, not as the defining scope of the lab.

::: {.grid}

::: {.g-col-md-4}
::: {.laser-card}
#### Process & Industrial Systems
- Separation processes (membrane, adsorption, distillation)
- Reactor design, modeling, and scale-up
- Plant-wide operation and optimization
- Electrification of chemical processes
- Process intensification
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-md-4}
::: {.laser-card}
#### Electrochemical & Energy Systems
- Electrochemical CO₂ reduction
- Green and blue hydrogen production
- Fuel cell and battery process modeling
- Oxy-combustion systems
- Energy system integration and optimization
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-md-4}
::: {.laser-card}
#### Bio/Bioprocess Systems
- Microalgae cultivation optimization
- Metabolic and kinetic process modeling
- Photobioreactor design and operation
- Biomass and lipid production
- Downstream bioprocess integration
:::
:::

:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Sustainability assessment as a validation layer:** TEA and LCA are embedded in our iterative workflow as decision validators and constraint definers. See [Pillar 3](#pillar-3-autonomous-operation-validation) above. They are not listed here as a standalone application area.
:::

---

## Research Facilities

LASER's infrastructure supports the full iterative workflow — from data collection and experiment sequencing through model deployment and reproducibility verification.

**Computational Resources**

- High-performance computing access for large-scale simulation and optimization
- Python-based in-house workflow code for sequential experimental design and surrogate modeling
- Aspen Plus, MATLAB, and open-source platforms (Pyomo, scikit-learn, IDAES ecosystem)

**Experimental Infrastructure**

- Pilot-scale reactor and separation systems
- Electrochemical cell setup for CO₂ reduction studies
- Photobioreactor for microalgae cultivation and characterization

**Reproducibility Infrastructure**

- Versioned experiment logs and dataset archives for completed studies
- Benchmark problem definitions for autonomous learning and DOE evaluation
- Workflow code documentation and reusable protocol libraries
- Resources available via collaboration are noted explicitly; nothing is claimed beyond actual access

---

## Selected Outputs

### Representative Publications

Selected peer-reviewed publications are listed on the [Publications page](publications.qmd). Ongoing and recent work spans:

- Sequential and Bayesian experimental design for process systems
- Hybrid surrogate modeling with uncertainty quantification
- TEA/LCA-integrated process design and optimization
- Process monitoring and fault detection in bioprocess systems

### Representative Projects

Project descriptions, funding sources, and collaboration details are available on the [Projects page](projects.qmd).

---

::: {.callout-tip}
## Interested in Collaboration?
We welcome research collaborations and industry partnerships. Please [contact us](about.qmd#contact) for more information.
:::
